stories.headline: Gov. Polis cuts Tina Peters' prison sentence in half, orders release June 1
stories.summary:
Gov. Jared Polis on Friday commuted the sentence of former Mesa County Clerk Tina Peters from nine years to four years and four and a half months, ordering her release on parole June 1. Peters was convicted of orchestrating a security breach of her county's election system in 2021 in a failed attempt to uncover voter fraud, and had become a national figure among election conspiracy theorists.

Polis said he was not pardoning Peters and that she remains a convicted felon, but argued her original sentence was "unduly harsh" due to her "incorrect and unpopular speech." The governor brushed off the political consequences of the decision, saying he tries to do what is right regardless of popularity.

The move drew immediate backlash from Democrats and many Republicans alike. Matt Crane, a Republican who leads the Colorado Clerks Association and the former Arapahoe County clerk, said the decision "signals that it's open season on our elections and election officials." President Trump posted "FREE TINA!" on social media after the announcement.

The clemency decision, announced two days after Colorado's legislative session ended, is widely seen as potentially ending Polis' viability as a future national Democratic candidate. Polis is term-limited and cannot seek reelection as governor in 2026.

stories.headline: Suspect in Denver five-alarm apartment construction fire found mentally incompetent to stand trial
stories.summary:
Tony Becerra, 38, who faces 27 arson charges and one burglary charge in connection with a January fire that destroyed a Denver apartment complex under construction, has been found mentally incompetent to stand trial, the Denver District Attorney's Office confirmed Thursday. The ruling means Becerra cannot understand court proceedings or assist in his own defense.

Under current Colorado law, defendants found incompetent to proceed and unlikely to be restored to competency must have their charges dismissed. Becerra is due back in court for a status conference on July 16.

The five-alarm fire occurred Jan. 2 at the Harker Heights apartment complex at Leetsdale Drive and South Forest Street. Becerra was arrested Jan. 13 after investigators used security cameras, facial recognition technology, and transit footage to trace his movements before and after the blaze.

Firefighters spent days battling the fire and used approximately 18 million gallons of water. Denver Fire Operations Division Chief Robert Murphy described it as the only five-alarm fire he had seen in 30 years with the department.

stories.headline: Colorado legislative session marked by budget cuts, veto threats, and modest policy wins
stories.summary:
Colorado's 2026 legislative session, the last of Gov. Jared Polis' tenure, concluded Wednesday after 120 days marked by a roughly $1 billion budget deficit that severely constrained lawmakers' ambitions. The session yielded fewer sweeping policy debates than prior years, with many consequential bills dying or being heavily amended amid fiscal constraints and internal Democratic disagreements.

Among the session's accomplishments were a resolution to a two-year debate over artificial intelligence regulation, a bipartisan measure to address the state's backlog of criminal defendants deemed incompetent to stand trial, and a package of housing bills aimed at affordability and transit infrastructure. Lawmakers also passed a bill requiring college campuses to stock abortion pills and banned pet stores from selling cats and dogs.

Philosophical differences between Polis and Democratic majorities killed other efforts, with the governor threatening or issuing vetoes on multiple fronts. Polis is expected to set a personal record for vetoes during his tenure, according to lawmakers.

House Speaker Julie McCluskie acknowledged the difficult environment: "We tackled the must-fix issues facing us. And we did it all with federal attacks and a billion-dollar deficit nipping at our heels."

stories.headline: Colorado legislature passes disability rights protections for students amid federal rollbacks
stories.summary:
The Colorado General Assembly passed Senate Bill 125 in the final hours of the 2026 session, codifying civil rights protections for students with disabilities into state law. The legislation requires school districts to ensure equal access to programs and services, establish a disability rights grievance process, and provide transportation and education to every student with a disability regardless of severity.

Democrats designed the bill in response to Trump administration changes to the federal Office for Civil Rights, which closed seven of its 12 regional offices and eliminated nearly 300 employees. Those offices have historically processed complaints from parents who believe their school is discriminating against their child.

Lawmakers considered nearly 60 education-related bills during the session, but several fell short, including proposals to fund teacher housing, reduce the time students spend on standardized testing, and establish state rules for a federal tax credit scholarship program. Sponsors of the failed bills said they plan to reintroduce the measures next year.

The disability rights bill represents one of the more significant education policy outcomes of a session constrained by a roughly $1 billion budget deficit.

stories.headline: Colorado braces for severe storms and possible snow after warm spring stretch
stories.summary:
A sharp weather shift is expected across Colorado this weekend, with the National Weather Service forecasting severe thunderstorms, rain showers, and even a chance of snow showers after a stretch of warm spring temperatures on the Front Range and Eastern Plains. NWS Boulder meteorologist Dave Barjenbruch described the swing as "classic Colorado."

The greatest risk for severe weather Saturday is just east of Denver and over northeast Colorado, though there is a slight chance of a strong thunderstorm or two in the metro area. Rain showers could affect Sunday's Colfax Marathon, which drew more than 28,000 registered runners.

Colorado's mountains could see several inches of snow starting Sunday night, while the foothills may see up to an inch. There is roughly a 50-50 chance of snowflakes in the Denver metro on Monday, though warm enough temperatures should prevent significant accumulation.

Temperatures could dip to or just below freezing Monday night, raising concerns about frost on early gardens. Forecasters said a hard freeze is unlikely for most of the metro area, but urged residents to monitor conditions.

stories.headline: Denver's housing market settles into stability with median close price holding near $605,000
stories.summary:
Denver's real estate market has moved away from its historically volatile seasonal patterns toward steady, predictable conditions, according to the Denver Metro Association of Realtors' April Market Trends Report. The median close price for April was $605,000, virtually unchanged from $604,000 in April 2025 and $602,000 in April 2024.

April recorded 3,926 closed sales, up 2 percent from March, while inventory grew significantly — active listings hit 11,539, a 17 percent increase month-over-month. Despite rising inventory giving buyers more negotiating power, well-priced homes continued to move quickly, with median days on market falling to 14 from 16 in March.

The average closed price edged up 0.53 percent year-over-year to $724,057, and the close-to-list price ratio remained high at 99.4 percent. Year-to-date closings dropped about 4 percent compared to 2025 but closely align with 2023 and 2024 figures, indicating a steady rather than contracting market.

Realtor analysts flagged rising energy costs as a variable to watch, noting that higher gas and energy prices could push qualified buyers out of the market and prompt demand for more energy-efficient homes later in 2026.

stories.headline: Belcaro Shopping Center apartment plan advances with concept approval from Denver
stories.summary:
A scaled-back plan by Kentro Group and Embrey to build 347 apartments at the Belcaro Shopping Center in Denver has cleared concept approval from the city. The project represents a reduction from earlier proposals for the site, which anchors a King Soopers-anchored retail center in the established Belcaro neighborhood.

Concept approval is an early step in Denver's development review process, allowing the project to move forward toward more detailed design and permitting review. The site is part of a broader wave of redevelopment activity targeting aging suburban shopping centers across the Denver metro area.

Details on project timelines, design specifics, and any community input processes were not immediately available from the Denver Business Journal report.

stories.headline: Broncos hire MLS stadium veteran to lead Empower Field at Mile High operations
stories.summary:
The Denver Broncos have brought on a new executive with Major League Soccer stadium experience to oversee day-to-day operations at Empower Field at Mile High, the Denver Business Journal reported Friday. The hire is intended to strengthen the management of one of the NFL's prominent stadium venues.

Details about the new executive's identity and specific prior MLS experience were not fully disclosed in available reporting, but the Broncos indicated the individual will be charged with leading all operational functions at the stadium.

Empower Field at Mile High hosts not only Broncos games but also major concerts, soccer matches, and other large-scale events throughout the year, making venue operations a significant year-round business for the franchise.

stories.headline: Colorado public libraries see 25% drop in book challenges in 2025
stories.summary:
Colorado public libraries recorded 43 challenges to books, materials, events, and exhibits in 2025, down 25 percent from 60 challenges in 2024, according to data from the Colorado Department of Education's Library Research Service. The decrease follows years of rapidly escalating challenge attempts, including a 500 percent increase between 2021 and 2022.

The decline runs counter to national trends. The American Library Association documented attempts to censor more than 4,200 unique titles nationwide in 2025, nearly 2,000 more than in 2024, with about 91.7 percent of challenged titles targeted by politically driven organizations.

Library advocates credited Colorado's 2024 law, signed by Gov. Polis, which required libraries to establish written policies for acquiring, retaining, and handling requests to remove books or other resources. Nederland Community Library director Elektra Greer said the early data from 2025 shows the value of citizen advocacy.

Not all Colorado library districts followed the statewide trend. Mesa County Libraries recorded 21 challenges in 2025, up sharply from two in 2024, though 14 of those were focused on a single item in a community display.

stories.headline: Eaton's Delaney Reuter wins 3A 3200-meter title a year after heartbreaking DQ at state meet
stories.summary:
Delaney Reuter, a junior at Eaton High School, claimed the girls' 3A 3200-meter state championship Thursday at Jeffco Stadium with a winning time of 10:41.95 — more than 30 seconds ahead of the second-place finisher — one year after she was disqualified from the same race for inadvertently cutting into the wrong lane.

In the 2025 state championship, Reuter finished what she believed was a winning race, only to learn she and five other runners had been disqualified for a lane violation. The runner who received the gold as a result, Alamosa's Elizabeth McQuitty, offered Reuter her medal out of sportsmanship; Reuter declined, choosing to earn it cleanly.

This year, Reuter said she thought about the possibility of repeating the mistake heading into the race, but executed cleanly from the start, calculating when she crossed the cut-line before accelerating away from the field. McQuitty and her parents attended Thursday's race and sent congratulations after the win.

"Felt like I have a cleanness. A clean slate," Reuter said after the race. Eaton distance coach Jessica Ruff said watching Reuter cross the finish line and collapse confirmed the significance of the moment.

stories.headline: I-25 North project brings new lane closures and $75 speed fines in work zone starting this week
stories.summary:
The Colorado Department of Transportation's I-25 North Express Lanes Project will bring a series of traffic impacts to the corridor between Mead and Berthoud beginning the week of May 17. Daily full closures of Weld County Road 32 under I-25 will resume Monday through Thursday from 7 a.m. to 5 p.m. and Friday from 7 a.m. to noon, as crews set new bridge girders.

CDOT has also activated $75 civil penalties for speeding in the I-25 North work zone as of April 2, following a 30-day grace period during which drivers received warning notices. The fines are part of Colorado's Speed Enforcement Program targeting high-risk areas including construction and school zones.

The I-25 East Frontage Road between WCR 32 and CO 56 remains temporarily closed to through traffic to allow for lane widening and roadway transition work. Motorists are directed to alternate north-south routes including WCR 9.5, WCR 7, and Colorado Boulevard.

Project operations will pause at noon Friday, May 22 and resume Tuesday, May 26 to minimize traffic delays over the Memorial Day weekend. Once a new High Plains Boulevard segment is completed, projected for summer 2026, frontage road traffic will be redirected.

stories.headline: Denver woman returns to Colfax Marathon five years after Stage 3 breast cancer diagnosis
stories.summary:
Denver resident Erlidawn Roy is set to run this weekend's Colfax Marathon, five years after a Stage 3 breast cancer diagnosis came just two days after her first race at the same event in 2021. Roy endured 16 rounds of chemotherapy and 21 rounds of radiation before gradually returning to running.

Roy, who began running on a treadmill in her garage before training outdoors at Harvey Park in southwest Denver, said the mindset that running instilled in her helped carry her through treatment. "Running has kind of mirrored that journey of fighting with cancer. You have to take it one step at a time, one movement at a time," she said.

She described the park as "sacred ground" for her family and said running has become a shared pursuit with her children and even her dog. Roy said she now marks her 2021 race as a victory despite the diagnosis that followed.

"Now it's just a thankfulness that I am there, cause there were points where I didn't think I would be on this earth anymore," Roy said. More than 28,000 runners participated in this year's Colfax Marathon across eight race formats on Saturday and Sunday.

weather.notable: About 10 degrees above the average high for this time of year!

joke.joke: First the doctor told me the good news: I was going to have a disease named after me.

topStory.headline: Trump Targets Kentucky Rep. Massie in Primary as GOP Weighs Cost of Dissent
topStory.summary:
Rep. Thomas Massie of Kentucky faces the most difficult reelection campaign of his career ahead of next Tuesday's Republican primary, after President Donald Trump made a rare personal trip to the state to campaign against him and recruited a challenger, former Navy SEAL Ed Gallrein, to run in his place. Trump has publicly called Massie a "moron" and a "nut job," labeling him "the WORST Republican Congressman," and has deployed senior advisors to support Gallrein's campaign in a district Trump carried by 35 points in 2024.

Massie drew Trump's wrath by voting against the president's signature tax legislation, citing concerns over adding to the national debt, by pushing for the release of Jeffrey Epstein files, and by opposing Trump's decision to pursue military action against Iran. Massie has argued that his positions are consistent with the America First principles Trump championed during his initial campaign, and has retained support from state-level Republicans, including state Sen. Gex Williams, who told party members at a Lincoln Day Dinner in Covington that voters can support both Trump and Massie simultaneously.

The Kentucky race is the most high-profile front in a broader effort by Trump to remove Republican dissenters from office. The president successfully pushed out several Indiana state legislators who opposed his redistricting plan last week, and is also backing a primary challenger against U.S. Sen. Bill Cassidy of Louisiana. Massie himself acknowledged to the Associated Press that the upcoming contest is "by far the most challenging reelection I've ever faced."

Republican voters in Kentucky's 4th Congressional District, which runs from the Louisville suburbs along the Ohio River through the Cincinnati exurbs and into Appalachian coal country, are divided. Some attendees at the Lincoln Day Dinner expressed unease about a party that punishes independent thinking, while others feel bound by loyalty to the president. The outcome will serve as a significant test of whether Trump's ability to enforce party discipline has grown since 2020, when the same district ignored his earlier calls to remove Massie from office.
